---
title: "DATA3888 Group Report"
author:
        - name: "Finance Group 21"
          affiliation: "The University of Sydney"

date: "`r format(Sys.time(), '%d %B, %Y')`"
format:
  html:
    code-tools: true
    code-fold: true
    fig_caption: yes
    number_sections: yes
    embed-resources: true
    theme: 
      light: lux
      dark: darkly
    css:
      - https://use.fontawesome.com/releases/v5.0.6/css/all.css
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 4
    toc_float: true
bibliography: references.bib
reference-section-title: "References"
execute:
  echo: true
  tidy: true
---

## Executive Summary

**Background:** Volatility forecasting sits at the core of options pricing and risk management. The Black-Scholes model, the foundation of modern derivatives pricing, requires a reliable estimate of future volatility that historical averages alone cannot reliably provide. Using high-frequency limit order book data provided by Optiver, we investigate whether a heterogeneous ensemble of forecasting models can systematically outperform classical benchmarks for 2-minute-ahead realised volatility prediction 

**Methodology:** To do so, we evaluate four models: The GARCH family as a classical benchmark, HAR-X as an augmented industry baseline, and two ensemble approaches: a Residual Stacker with QLike-optimised correction and a Graph Neural Network (GNN) with a learned HAR blend. These models are assessed on RMSPE and QLike loss metrics, which together capture both point accuracy and the asymmetric cost of forecast errors relevant to options pricing. 

**Findings:** The results demonstrate that ensemble methods improve upon classical benchmarks, though the nature of improvement depends on the objective: the Residual Stacker achieves the lowest QLike (0.045), outperforming all benchmarks including HAR-X (0.051) and GARCH (0.055), while the GNN Hybrid achieves the lowest RMSPE (0.417), a \~15% reduction over HAR-X(0.445), but at the cost of elevated QLike (0.072). No single ensemble dominates across both metrics.

**Conclusion:** These results suggest that model selection in volatility forecasting cannot be reduced to a single metric and the appropriate ensemble depends on whether the downstream task penalises miscalibration or magnitude error. To support reproducibility and practical evaluation, the analysis is accompanied by an interactive Plotly Dash monitoring tool enabling inspection of model predictions across stocks and per-stock risk decomposition, facilitating translation of forecast methodology into applied risk workflows.  

## Background

### History

At the core of options pricing lies a fundamental challenge: the Black-Scholes model requires a forward-looking volatility estimate, yet future volatility remains inherently unobservable. GARCH-family models addressed this by inferring conditional volatility from daily return sequences, establishing the early standard for volatility forecasting [@ghysels2006predicting]. The availability of high-frequency limit order book data, however, made more granular approaches possible.

Corsi formalised this with the Heterogeneous Autoregressive model of Realised Volatility (HAR-RV), which regresses future realised volatility on lagged RV at multiple horizons, capturing the heterogeneous time scales at which different market participants operate. [@corsi2009har] Despite its parsimony, HAR-RV has proven consistently difficult to outperform out-of-sample, cementing its status as the de facto industry benchmark. For Optiver, a global electronic market maker pricing and hedging options in real time, it represents the practical baseline against which any proposed improvement must be evaluated.

### Motivation & Research Question

HAR-RV's limitations are well-documented, yet its out-of-sample robustness has proven remarkably difficult to overcome. Rather than replacing HAR, this study investigates whether heterogeneous ensemble methods can exploit HAR's robustness as a foundation. To do so, the ensemble methods extend it with nonlinear correction and cross-stock structure to capture signals that a linear, stock-independent model cannot. Both ensemble approaches proposed here are architecturally constrained to improve on HAR rather than circumvent it. As such, any measured gain is more plausibly attributable to what the ensemble adds beyond the baseline. This motivates the central research question:

*Can a heterogeneous ensemble of volatility forecasting models systematically outperform individual classical benchmarks for short-term volatility forecasting using high-frequency order book data?*

### Exploratory Data Analysis

```{python}
#| label: fig-eda-target-distribution
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of log-realised volatility across all forecast windows."
#| echo: false

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.image as mpimg

img = mpimg.imread("../eda_pictures/eda_target_distribution.png")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.imshow(img)
ax.axis("off")

plt.show()
```

The Optiver dataset covers 112 stocks at per-second resolution, with the study splitting each 10-minute window into an 8-minute input period and a 2-minute forecast target.

**Finding 1: Cross-stock heterogeneity is extreme.** Per-stock summary statistics show that mean bid-ask spreads range across three orders of magnitude and mean per-second volume ranges from double digits to tens of thousands. A spread of 0.001 on a stock with WAP near 1.0 is economically very different from the same spread on a stock trading at a different price level. This makes per-stock model fitting and within-stock z-scoring of features essential before any pooled or cross-stock modelling.

**Finding 2: Log-RV is approximately Gaussian with stock-level shifts.** The forecast target, $log⁡RV$ computed over seconds 480–599, has a pooled mean of –6.612 and standard deviation of 0.709 across 343,145 windows. Only 7 windows (0.002%) have exactly zero price movement, and roughly 0.1% of windows fall below the noise floor of $log(10^{-4}) = -9.21$. The near-Gaussian shape after log transformation supports OLS fitting in log-space (as HAR and HAR-X do) and means the noise floor affects a negligible fraction of observations.

**Finding 3: Volatility co-moves strongly across stocks, but contemporaneously rather than predictively.** Four cross-stock tests were run on the $112 \times 3064$ panel of $logRV$

-   Pairwise Pearson correlation across all 6,216 stock pairs yields a mean of 0.672 with 100% of pairs positive and 97% above 0.5, an unusually strong level of co-movement.

-   Permutation testing (100 shuffles of stock labels within each `time_id`) confirms this is genuine structure: the real mean correlation (0.672) exceeds the null mean (0.519) with a z-score of 7,230 $(p \approx 0 )$.

-   Granger causality (lag = 3, 200 randomly sampled directed pairs), however, finds only 0.5% of pairs significant at $\alpha = 0.05$, which is \*below\* the 5% expected by chance. A binomial test yields $p=1.0$, confirming no excess predictive causality.

-   Lead-lag correlation reinforces this: same-time correlation is strong (mean = 0.670), but lagged correlation is effectively zero (mean = –0.012, no pairs above \|0.1\|).

These findings motivate models that can capture both cross-sectional structure and nonlinear microstructure effects.

## Methodology

**ADD WORKFLOW DIAGRAM**

### Preprocessing

From the raw order book, six features are computed per second: WAP, log return, bid-ask spread, total volume, price spread, and depth imbalance. Since some seconds contain no order book updates, the data is forward-filled to maintain a continuous one-second grid before feature computation. The forecast target is defined as:

$$
\text{RV} = \sqrt{\sum r_t^2}
$$

over seconds 480–599, with the model trained to predict $logRV$, floored at $10⁻⁴$. Five-fold cross-validation is constructed using GroupShuffleSplit on `time_id` (80/20 split).

### Evaluation Strategy

All models are evaluated under 5-fold cross-validation with GroupKFold on stock_id, where each fold holds out a distinct subset of stocks completely unseen during training since `time_id` is non-sequential and arbitrary, grouping by stock_id ensures the split tests cross-stock generalization rather than temporal extrapolation. Out-of-fold predictions are concatenated across all five folds to produce a single set of predictions covering the entire dataset, on which RMSPE and QLike are computed. Pairwise Diebold–Mariano tests with Newey-West HAC variance estimation (lag truncation $h = n^{1/3}$) are conducted on these out-of-fold residuals across all model combinations under both metrics. Benjamini–Hochberg FDR correction is applied to the resulting 12 simultaneous tests, preferred over Bonferroni given the shared data and fold structure, keeping only the significant results after correction.

```{python}
#| label: fig-rmpse-vs-qlike
#| fig-cap: "RMSPE and QLike loss functions plotted against the forecast-to-realised volatility ratio. RMSPE penalises under- and overprediction symmetrically, while QLike penalises underprediction more heavily, reflecting the higher cost of underestimating risk."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

r = np.linspace(0.05, 4.0, 500)
rmspe = np.abs(1 - r)
qlike = 1/r + np.log(r) - 1

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6), dpi=200)
fig.patch.set_facecolor('white')

ax.plot(
    r, rmspe,
    color='#3266ad',
    linewidth=2.5,
    label='RMSPE  |1 − r|'
)

ax.plot(
    r, qlike,
    color='#d85a30',
    linewidth=2.5,
    linestyle='--',
    label='QLIKE  1/r + ln(r) − 1'
)


ax.axvline(
    x=1,
    color='grey',
    linewidth=0.8,
    linestyle=':'
)

ax.set_xlim(0, 4)
ax.set_ylim(0, 5)
ax.set_xlabel(r'r = $F_t$ / $RV_t$', fontsize=13)
ax.set_ylabel('Per-observation loss', fontsize=13)
ax.set_title(
    'RMSPE vs QLIKE Loss Functions for Realised Volatility',
    fontsize=14,
    pad=20
)

fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.1, right=0.98, top=0.90, bottom=0.18)

ax.legend(frameon=False, fontsize=10, loc='upper right')

ax.text(
    0.25, 0.7,
    '← Underprediction',
    transform=ax.transAxes,
    fontsize=11,
    color='grey',
    ha='center'
)

ax.text(
    0.75, 0.7,
    'Overprediction →',
    transform=ax.transAxes,
    fontsize=11,
    color='grey',
    ha='center'
)

ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax.grid(alpha=0.15)
plt.show()
```

Two metrics are used in combination, as neither alone is sufficient. RMSPE, defined as $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{y_i-\hat{y}_i}{y_i}\right)^2}$ , measures proportional prediction error symmetrically. QLike, defined as $\frac{\sigma}{\hat{\sigma}} - \log\left(\frac{\sigma}{\hat{\sigma}}\right) - 1$ , penalises under-prediction more heavily, reflecting the asymmetric cost of underestimating risk in options pricing. [@patton2011volatility]. Although a low QLike can be achieved by a model that systematically overpredicts, due to the fact the loss function is lenient toward overestimation; it does not, on its own, indicate forecast accuracy. RMSPE is therefore needed to confirm that predictions are close to realised values, while QLike ensures that residual errors lean toward the conservative side. The preferred model is one that achieves both low RMSPE and low QLike. 

### Benchmark Models

#### GARCH

The Generalised Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity (GARCH) family acts as a classical benchmark for forecasting volatility. These GARCH models work by estimating the conditional variance of returns given the historical market information [@engle_garch101; @vlab_garch]. These models are still used widely in industry due to their ability to deal with volatility clusters and heteroskedasticity within financial return data [@engle_garch101]. Despite the emergence of machine learning and other realised volatility approaches, GARCH remains a standard benchmark for which other more complex models are evaluated against.

The GARCH model used in this study is a GARCH(1,1) specification, fitted independently per (`stock_id`, `time_id`), using logarithmic returns derived from Weighted Average Price (WAP). The model works by taking the first 480 seconds of returns as inputs and fits a GARCH(1,1) specification onto it, following the formula:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \sigma_{t-1}^2 + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2 $$

where $\varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t$ and $z_t$ is a standard noise process. [@vlab_garch]

Once fitted, the model produces a multi-step forecast over the next 120 seconds by projecting the conditional variance forward recursively, which is then aggregated to produce a single realised-volatility estimate.

#### HAR-X

HAR-X serves as the augmented industry baseline, extending the HAR-RV framework of Corsi (2009) by incorporating order-book microstructure features alongside realised volatility lags [@corsi2009har]. The model regresses log-realised volatility over the forecast window on three heterogeneous RV components: the full 8-minute input window, the final 2-minute bucket, and the intermediate 4-minute window which translates volatility persistence across intraday horizons following Andersen et al. (2003). [@andersen2003realized]

A meaningful ensemble evaluation requires a baseline that already exploits the most accessible predictive signal. By augmenting HAR with bid-ask spread, total order-book volume, and volume imbalance, HAR-X absorbs the straightforward gains from microstructure information that a pure RV model cannot access. Any further improvement achieved by the ensemble approaches therefore reflects nonlinear or cross-stock structure that a linear, stock-independent model cannot capture making HAR-X a more rigorous point of comparison than HAR alone.

The model is estimated via OLS in log-volatility space with predictions exponentiated back to the RV scale, consistent with the log-normal treatment adopted throughout.

### Ensemble Models

Given the strength of HAR-X as a linear baseline, we now introduce two ensemble approaches designed to extend it in complementary ways.

#### Residual Stacker with QLike-optimised correction

The Residual Stacker is a two-stage ensemble that augments the HAR-RV baseline with a LightGBM residual correction layer. It was selected in this study for its demonstrated speed advantage over XGBoost and **GBDT** alternatives at equivalent predictive accuracy [@ke2017lightgbm]. In the first stage, per-stock HAR predictions are generated via nested 5-fold cross validation to produce out-of-fold residuals on a training set. This acts as a stacking design that prevents data leakage in the correction stage. In the second stage, LightGBM is trained to predict these residuals using 42 engineered features derived from the order book, selected via a three-stage pipeline. This consists of **mutual information filtering**, SHAP-based importance ranking, and permutation importance, spanning realised volatility lags, bid-ask spread dynamics, return distribution moments, and cross-stock z-scores [@wang2024feature]. The final prediction combines both stages:

$$
\hat{y} = \exp(\log \sigma_{\text{HAR}} + f_{\text{LGB}}(x))
$$

The correction model is then trained directly on a custom QLike objective with analytic gradient and Hessian. This architecture is designed so that any improvement over HAR primarily reflects the nonlinear structure in the residuals that OLS cannot capture, isolating the contribution of machine learning to volatility forecasting.

#### Spatio-Temporal GNN with learned HAR blend

The spatio-temporal GNN addresses HAR's core limitation: it treats every stock independently. In reality, volatility shocks co-move across stocks, and spatio-temporal GNNs have demonstrated superior performance over traditional models in cross-sectional time series forecasting by explicitly modelling both spatial dependencies between nodes and temporal dynamics within each node [@alshahil2023stgnn].

The model encodes each stock's 8-bucket sequence using a **Mamba** selective state-space temporal encoder, then constructs a dynamic learned graph where each stock attends to its top ten most relevant neighbours via **GATv2** message passing. A cross-stock attention layer captures any residual market-wide co-movement that the **sparse graph** misses.

A linear HAR branch runs in parallel, and a **sigmoid-gated scalar** weight w blends the two paths:

$$
\hat{y} = w \cdot \hat{y}_{\text{HAR}} + (1 - w) \cdot \hat{y}_{\text{GNN}}
$$

If the GNN contributes nothing, w converges to 1 and the model reduces to HAR. This makes the architecture self-regularising and directly answers the research question: any improvement over the benchmark is attributable to the cross-stock structure the GNN adds. Hence, the GNN does not replace the HAR model, it is constrained to improve upon it.

## Results

```{python}
#| label: fig-model-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Out-of-sample forecast evaluation across models. QLIKE and RMSPE computed on realised variance. Brackets denote Diebold-Mariano significance vs HAR-X (*** p < 0.01)."

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mtick
from pathlib import Path

BASE_DIR = Path.cwd().parent
EVAL_DIR = BASE_DIR / "outputs" / "evaluation"

dm_df = pd.read_csv(EVAL_DIR / "dm_tests_all_models_corrected.csv")
base = pd.read_csv(EVAL_DIR / "base_predictions.csv")

model_names = ["HAR-X", "QLIKE-LGB", "GNN", "GARCH"]
colors      = ["steelblue", "seagreen", "tomato", "sienna"]
EPS         = 1e-10

required_cols = ["actual_rv"] + [f"pred_{m}" for m in model_names]
missing = [c for c in required_cols if c not in base.columns]
if missing:
    raise ValueError(f"Missing columns in base: {missing}")

actual = base["actual_rv"].values

def rmspe(y, p):
    y = np.maximum(y, EPS)
    p = np.maximum(p, EPS)
    return np.sqrt(np.mean(((y - p) / y) ** 2))

def qlike(y, p):
    y = np.maximum(y, EPS)
    p = np.maximum(p, EPS)
    return np.mean(y / p - np.log(y / p) - 1)

rmspe_vals = []
qlike_vals = []
for m in model_names:
    pred = base[f"pred_{m}"].values
    rmspe_vals.append(rmspe(actual, pred))
    qlike_vals.append(qlike(actual, pred))

def get_star(metric, m1, m2):
    row = dm_df[
        (dm_df["metric"] == metric) &
        (((dm_df["model_1"] == m1) & (dm_df["model_2"] == m2)) |
         ((dm_df["model_1"] == m2) & (dm_df["model_2"] == m1)))
    ]
    if row.empty:
        return ""
    return row["sig"].iloc[0]

def bracket_base(values, pad=1.10):
    return max(values) * pad

def add_bracket(ax, x1, x2, y, text, arm_frac=0.015):
    arm = y * arm_frac
    ax.plot(
        [x1, x1, x2, x2],
        [y, y + arm, y + arm, y],
        color="black", lw=1, clip_on=False,
    )
    ax.text(
        (x1 + x2) / 2, y + arm * 1.5,
        text, ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=13,
    )

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))
x         = np.arange(len(model_names))
harx_idx  = model_names.index("HAR-X")

axes[0].bar(x, qlike_vals, color=colors, alpha=0.8)
axes[0].set_title("QLIKE")
for i, v in enumerate(qlike_vals):
    axes[0].text(i, v, f"{v:.4f}", ha="center", va="bottom")

best_q = int(np.argmin(qlike_vals))
sig_q  = get_star("QLIKE", "HAR-X", model_names[best_q])
if sig_q:
    add_bracket(axes[0], harx_idx, best_q, bracket_base(qlike_vals), sig_q)

axes[0].set_xticks(x)
axes[0].set_xticklabels(model_names)
axes[0].tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=8)
axes[0].yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FormatStrFormatter("%.3f"))
axes[0].set_ylim(0, max(qlike_vals) * 1.22)

axes[1].bar(x, rmspe_vals, color=colors, alpha=0.8)
axes[1].set_title("RMSPE")
for i, v in enumerate(rmspe_vals):
    axes[1].text(i, v, f"{v:.4f}", ha="center", va="bottom")

best_r = int(np.argmin(rmspe_vals))
sig_r  = get_star("RMSPE", "HAR-X", model_names[best_r])
if sig_r:
    add_bracket(axes[1], harx_idx, best_r, bracket_base(rmspe_vals), sig_r)

axes[1].set_xticks(x)
axes[1].set_xticklabels(model_names)
axes[1].tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=8)
axes[1].yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FormatStrFormatter("%.3f"))
axes[1].set_ylim(0, max(rmspe_vals) * 1.22)

plt.tight_layout()
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.12, wspace=0.35)
plt.show()
```

The results show that model performance is not uniformly improved by greater complexity. HAR-X remains the most interpretable and balanced reference point because it improves on HAR by adding exogenous liquidity and order-book variables, achieving an RMSPE of 0.5416 and QLike of 0.0511. This suggests that short-horizon volatility is shaped not only by volatility persistence, but also by immediate trading frictions such as spread, volume, and order imbalance.

```{python}
#| label: fig-forecast-stability
#| fig-cap: "5-fold forecast stability. The confidence intervals show that the QLIKE-LGB and GNN advantages are stable across folds. Points show fold-level estimates, filled circles show the mean, and horizontal bars span the 95% t-interval. Dashed line marks the HAR-X mean for reference."
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.lines as mlines
from scipy import stats
from pathlib import Path
import json

BASE_DIR = Path.cwd().parent
EVAL_DIR = BASE_DIR / "outputs" / "evaluation"

with open(EVAL_DIR / "fold_metrics.json") as f:
    fold_metrics = json.load(f)
fold_qlike_metrics = fold_metrics["qlike"]
fold_rmspe_metrics = fold_metrics["rmspe"]

model_names = ["HAR-X", "QLIKE-LGB", "GNN", "GARCH"]
colors      = ["steelblue", "seagreen", "tomato", "sienna"]

def compute_ci(fold_values, confidence=0.95):
    arr  = np.array(fold_values)
    mean = arr.mean()
    se   = stats.sem(arr)
    ci   = stats.t.interval(confidence, df=len(arr) - 1, loc=mean, scale=se)
    return mean, ci[0], ci[1]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4))

for ax, (metric_name, fold_data) in zip(axes, [
    ("QLIKE", fold_qlike_metrics),
    ("RMSPE", fold_rmspe_metrics),
]):
    y_pos = np.arange(len(model_names))

    for i, (name, color) in enumerate(zip(model_names, colors)):
        mean, lo, hi = compute_ci(fold_data[name])
        fold_vals    = fold_data[name]
        ax.plot([lo, hi], [i, i], color=color,
                linewidth=3, alpha=0.8, solid_capstyle="round", zorder=2)
        ax.scatter(mean, i, color=color, s=120,
                   zorder=4, edgecolors="white", linewidth=1.5)
        ax.scatter(fold_vals, [i] * 5, color=color,
                   s=30, alpha=0.5, zorder=3, marker="|")

    ax.set_yticks(y_pos)
    ax.set_yticklabels(model_names, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel(metric_name, fontsize=11)
    ax.set_title(metric_name, fontsize=11, fontweight="bold")
    ax.grid(axis="x", alpha=0.3, zorder=1)
    ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
    ax.invert_yaxis()

    harx_mean, _, _ = compute_ci(fold_data["HAR-X"])
    ax.axvline(harx_mean, color="steelblue", linewidth=1,
               linestyle="--", alpha=0.5, label=f"HAR-X mean ({harx_mean:.4f})")
    ax.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.6)

    ax.figure.canvas.draw()
    x_range = ax.get_xlim()[1] - ax.get_xlim()[0]
    for i, (name, color) in enumerate(zip(model_names, colors)):
        mean, lo, hi = compute_ci(fold_data[name])
        ax.text(hi + x_range * 0.02, i, f"{mean:.4f}",
                va="center", ha="left", fontsize=8.5,
                color=color, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xlim(right=ax.get_xlim()[1] + x_range * 0.12)

handles = [
    mlines.Line2D([0], [0], color=c, linewidth=3,
                  marker="o", markersize=8,
                  markerfacecolor=c, markeredgecolor="white",
                  label=n)
    for c, n in zip(colors, model_names)
]
handles.append(
    mlines.Line2D([0], [0], color="grey", linewidth=0,
                  marker="|", markersize=10, label="Fold estimates")
)
fig.legend(handles=handles, loc="lower center", ncol=5,
           fontsize=9, framealpha=0.7, bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, -0.08))

plt.tight_layout()
fig.subplots_adjust(bottom=0.18)
plt.show()
```

The ensemble results should be interpreted through the evaluation strategy. As discussed earlier, RMSPE measures proportional point error symmetrically, while QLike penalises volatility underprediction much more severely and is relatively more tolerant of overprediction. This explains why QLIKE-LGB achieves the lowest QLike at 0.0450 in the model comparison chart. The confidence-interval chart further shows that this QLike advantage is stable across the 5-fold cross-validation, while the Diebold-Mariano tests confirm that the improvement is statistically significant against HAR-X, GNN, and GARCH. Since QLIKE-LGB is trained using a QLike-optimised residual correction, it learns to avoid underestimating volatility, making it better suited to risk-sensitive options pricing. However, its weaker RMSPE shows that conservative calibration does not necessarily produce the closest proportional forecasts. 

By contrast, the GNN achieves the lowest RMSPE of 0.4121 in the performance chart, and the confidence-interval chart shows this advantage is also consistent across folds. This supports the predictive value of cross-stock volatility spillovers. Although the Diebold-Mariano tests also favour GNN under RMSPE, the comparison against GARCH should be interpreted cautiously because GARCH shows much wider fold-level variation. More importantly, the GNN’s weakest QLike of 0.0725 shows that closer average point forecasts can still be poorly calibrated when errors lean toward underprediction.

Overall, the results indicate a clear trade-off between point accuracy and risk-sensitive calibration: the GNN is most effective for minimising proportional forecast error, while the Residual Stacker is better suited to conservative volatility estimation, and HAR-X remains the most balanced and interpretable baseline.

#### Product

**(ADD IMAGE OF DASH APP maybe in appendix?)**

While the research question focuses on whether heterogeneous ensembles outperform classical volatility forecasting benchmarks, practitioners also need to understand when those improvements occur and whether they are robust across different stocks and market conditions. To make the results visualisable to the user, an interactive volatility monitoring dashboard was built using Plotly Dash, as illustrated in **Figure ?**. 

The Dash App helps explore the research question by allowing the user to compare the benchmark models (GARCH and HAR-X) to the ensemble models (GNN and Residual Stacker). By visualising the actual realised volatility alongside the model predictions, the dashboard highlights situations where ensemble methods provide superior forecasts compared to the benchmarks, revealing patterns to users that aggregate metrics alone cannot capture. This enables practitioners to interpret the models’ relative strengths and apply the most appropriate forecasting approach depending on the trading environment and risk priorities.

## Discussion

Several limitations are worth noting. GARCH was also fitted on only 480 seconds of returns per window, which likely put it at a disadvantage and may partly explain its weaker performance. Next, the time IDs themselves are non-sequential, so the ordering of windows cannot be treated as a clean time series. This limits the ability to model genuine intraday trends or carry information across windows in a principled way. Beyond that, the analysis covers a single dataset of 112 stocks at one forecast horizon, so it is unclear how well the findings carry over to other market conditions, or longer horizons. The GNN Hybrid also requires significant computation and is harder to interpret, which is a practical concern in fast-moving trading environments.

## Conclusion

To conclude, the findings provide strong evidence that heterogeneous ensemble methods can outperform classical benchmarks for short-term volatility forecasting using high-frequency order book data. The Residual Stacker reduces QLike by 11.8% over Base HAR and the GNN Hybrid reduces RMSPE by 29.3%, both statistically significant. High-frequency order book data contains predictive structure beyond what classical HAR captures, and ensemble methods are effective at extracting it. 

Future work could address several remaining gaps. Fitting GARCH over a longer window would make it a fairer benchmark. There is also a natural next step in combining the two ensembles, using the GNN's cross-stock signal for accuracy while applying the Residual Stacker's correction for calibration. Testing across more stocks, horizons, and market would also strengthen confidence in the results.

## Student Contributions

## Appendix {.appendix}
